name: Swami Karpatri
dates_notes: Born 1907 (Vikrama Saṃvat 1964); died 1982. Founder of the Akhil Bhāratīya Rāma-Rājya Pariṣad (1948); active in the Banaras pāṭhaśālā Advaita network. Daśanāmī Sannyāsī of the Sarasvatī sub-order.

dates_evidence.description: Government of India Registrar of Political Parties: Akhil Bhāratīya Rāma-Rājya Pariṣad founding records (1948); commemorative postage-stamp records.

core_thesis: Svāmī Karpātrī — born Har Nārāyaṇa Ojha, ordained Hariharānanda Sarasvatī in the Daśanāmī-Sarasvatī order, and popularly named for the *kar-pātra*, the cupped hand used as an alms-bowl — was the twentieth-century Banaras Advaitin who founded the Akhil Bhāratīya Rāma-Rājya Pariṣad in 1948 (so the Pariṣad's registration records and the Dharma Saṅgha publication colophons). His prose joins orthodox Advaita Vedānta to a public defense of *varṇāśrama-* and *sanātana-dharma*, programmatically stated in the opening chapters of the *Varṇāśrama-Maryādā* and *Mārksavāda aur Rāmarājya*. The output spans Advaita doctrine (*Vedārtha-Pārijāta*; the multi-volume *Vedānta-Mīmāṃsā*), *bhakti*-doctrine (*Saṅkīrtana-Mīmāṃsā*), Mīmāṃsā-grounded ritualism (*Varṇāśrama-Maryādā*), and political theology (*Mārksavāda aur Rāmarājya*). Doctrinally he stands in the post-Madhusūdana Vivaraṇa stream: the *Vedānta-Mīmāṃsā* (vol. 2) defends *mūlāvidyā* — *avidyā* as a positive substratum (*bhāvarūpa*) — against the Holenarsipur Śuddha-Śāṅkara revisionism of Satchidānandendra's *Mūlāvidyā-Nirāsa* (1929).

engaged_works.title: Vedartha-Parijata
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed; the work circulates in the Dharma Saṅgha and Karpātrī-affiliated publishing tradition as Karpātrī's own composition, but no critical edition has yet been produced.

ascription_evidence.description: Dharma Saṅgha and Karpātrī-affiliated publishing tradition.

engaged_works.summary: A Sanskrit *prakaraṇa* on the *artha* of the Veda. The treatise defends the orthodox Smārta-Mīmāṃsā position that the Veda's *karma-kāṇḍa* and *jñāna-kāṇḍa* are an integrated whole, with Vedāntic *jñāna* as the *tātparya* of the *jñāna-kāṇḍa* (cf. Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.4 *tattu samanvayāt* and Sureśvara's [*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.5–10](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.5-10) on the *karma–jñāna* integration; Karpātrī works within this framework). The work is constructed against modern reformist readings of the Veda — both Western philological-naturalist readings (Müller-school) and the Ārya Samāj reformulation (Dayananda's *Satyārtha-Prakāśa*, 1875, ch. 7) — and reasserts the *bhāṣyakāra* tradition's authority. The doctrinal substance is conservative Advaita Vedānta with extensive Mīmāṃsā-ritual grounding ([Jaimini's *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2) with Śabara's *bhāṣya*, the *codanā-lakṣaṇa* sūtra, supplies the ritual-Vedic frame the work assumes); the treatise's distinctive contribution is the integration of the two registers in defense of the modern *pāṭhaśālā* and *yajña* tradition.

engaged_works.title: Vedanta-Mimamsa series
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored multi-volume Hindi exposition with Sanskrit citation.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus; Dharma Saṅgha publication records.

engaged_works.summary: A multi-volume Hindi exposition of Advaita Vedānta with extensive Sanskrit citation (Dharma Saṅgha publication, multiple volumes 1950s–1970s). The work proceeds topic by topic through the school's standard agenda — ontology, epistemology, the *avidyā*-doctrine, the path of *jñāna*, the relation between *bhakti* and *jñāna*, *jīvanmukti* — and supplies the modern conservative Advaita position at each locus. The doctrinal alignment is with the post-Madhusūdana Vivaraṇa scholastic apparatus (Madhusūdana, *Advaita-Siddhi* prathama-paricheda; Prakāśātman, *Pañca-Pādikā-Vivaraṇa*); the *mūlāvidyā* doctrine — *avidyā* as a positive substratum (*bhāvarūpa*) located in Brahman — is defended (in *Vedānta-Mīmāṃsā* vol. 2) against Satchidānandendra's revisionism (*Mūlāvidyā-Nirāsa*, 1929, which denies *bhāvarūpa avidyā* and rejects the Vivaraṇa apparatus as un-Śāṅkaran). The work is the principal extended written record of Karpātrī's Advaita Vedānta and the natural reference for his doctrinal position on contested issues.

engaged_works.title: Sankirtana-Mimamsa

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus; Dharma Saṅgha publication records.

engaged_works.summary: A treatise on the *bhakti*-practice of *saṅkīrtana* (collective devotional chanting). The work integrates the Smārta-Advaita doctrinal position with the practice of devotional chanting that the Caitanya tradition had made famous in Bengal (Caitanya, *Śikṣāṣṭaka* vv. 1–3; Rūpa Gosvāmī, [*Bhakti-Rasāmṛta-Sindhu* 1.2.2](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.2.2) on *navadhā-bhakti*) and that was spreading through North India in Karpātrī's period. The treatise negotiates the tension between Advaita's standard subordination of *bhakti* to *jñāna* (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 3.4.26–27) and the practical importance of devotional chanting in modern orthodox Hindu life; Karpātrī's resolution is broadly Madhusūdana-style ([*Bhakti-Rasāyana* 1.1](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1), on *bhakti* as both *sādhana* and *phala*) — *bhakti* is treated as a preparatory and accompanying *sādhana* with its own intrinsic value, while not displacing *jñāna* as the proximate cause of *mokṣa* (cf. Madhusūdana, *Advaita-Siddhi* uttara-paricheda on *jīvanmukti*).

engaged_works.title: Varnashrama-Maryada

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus; Dharma Saṅgha publication records.

engaged_works.summary: A polemical Hindi treatise defending the orthodox *varṇāśrama-dharma* tradition against modern reformist critique. The doctrinal substance is dharma-śāstra (drawing on *Manu-Smṛti* 1.87–101 on the four-*varṇa* origin and *Manu-Smṛti* 6.1–96 on the *āśrama* sequence) and Mīmāṃsā argument ([Jaimini, *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* 6.1.25–38](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/6.1.25-38), on adhikāra restricted by *varṇa*); the political register is mid-twentieth-century post-independence India. Its substance is Mīmāṃsā-Smārta dharma-theory; the treatise is the principal written record of the orthodox Daśanāmī-Sarasvatī position on social order in the modern period, continuous with Karpātrī's Vedāntic doctrine. The treatise's principal interlocutors are the modern reformist movements — the Ārya Samāj (Dayananda, *Satyārtha-Prakāśa*, 1875, ch. 4 on *varṇāśrama* as merit-based rather than birth-based) and neo-Vedāntic universalism (Vivekananda, *Complete Works* vol. 3, on caste as a contingent social form) — that read *varṇāśrama* as a contingent social institution rather than as integral to *sanātana-dharma* in the orthodox Smārta reading.

engaged_works.title: Marxavad aur Ramrajya

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus; Dharma Saṅgha publication records.

engaged_works.summary: A Hindi treatise contrasting Marxism with the *Rāma-rājya* ideal of dharmic governance, written in the post-independence period when Marxist political organizing was a significant force in North Indian public life. The work bears on Karpātrī's public theology, which is doctrinally continuous with his Vedāntic position: the metaphysical reality of the Ātman (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1, against Buddhist *anātman*; Karpātrī invokes the same dialectic in *Mārksavāda aur Rāmarājya* against Marxist materialism), the moral order grounded in *dharma* (so the *dharma*-vocabulary of *Mahābhārata* 12.109 onward, the *Rāja-Dharma-Parvan*), and the political-theological centrality of the *Rāma-rājya* pattern as the practical realization of that order (drawing on Tulsīdās, *Rāmacarita-Mānasa*, *Uttara-Kāṇḍa*, on the *Rāma-rājya* utopia). The work is the principal documentary source for the political theology of the Akhil Bhāratīya Rāma-Rājya Pariṣad, the political organization Karpātrī founded in 1948 (so the Pariṣad's founding records).
